attrs.alt: Access package lifecycle states and delegation model: draft to published to active assignment to expired, with four delegation tiers

content.text: An access package is the central object in Entitlement Management — a named, manageable bundle of resource roles that can be requested, approved, assigned, and expired as a unit. This topic covers the operational details: what resources can be bundled, how multiple policies on one package work, how extension requests flow, and how the delegation model lets business units own their own access without needing tenant admin rights.
A single access package can contain resource roles from four supported originSystem types. Each requires the resource to be added to the catalog before it can be added to a package.
Adding an M365 group grants Teams channel access and SharePoint team site via group membership
App roles defined in the app manifest
Must be an app with app roles configured; user assignment required must be enabled
Site must be indexed by Entitlement Management; may take up to 24h to appear
Azure AD built-in directory role (via PIM)
Only supported when the catalog has Azure AD roles enabled and PIM is configured

You cannot add an Azure resource role (ARM RBAC) directly to an access package resource list. Use a PIM-eligible group instead: assign the Azure RBAC role to the group, and add the group to the access package.
Listing Available Resources in a Catalog
Multiple Policies on One Access Package

One access package can have multiple assignment policies. This is the standard pattern when the same bundle of resources needs to be available under different conditions — for example, automatic self-approval for senior employees but manager approval for contractors, or internal access with no expiry versus external access that expires in 30 days.

Users submit requests through myaccess.microsoft.com or via the Graph API. Before a request can be submitted, the user must provide a justification if the policy requires it. Approvers receive email with a direct link to the approval page.

When an assignment is approaching its expiration date, the user can request an extension before access lapses. The extension goes through the same approval flow as the original request, unless the policy sets isApprovalRequiredForExtension = false.

Setting isHidden = true on a package removes it from the public catalog browse view at myaccess.microsoft.com. Users can only request it if they navigate to the direct URL. This is useful for privileged packages you don't want visible to all users browsing the catalog.

# Hide an existing access package
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/accessPackages/<package-id>" \
  -Body '{"isHidden": true}' -ContentType 'application/json'

# Retrieve the direct access link for a hidden package
$pkg = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/accessPackages/<package-id>"

# Direct URL format for myaccess.microsoft.com
$tenantId = (Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization").value[0].id
Write-Host "Direct URL: https://myaccess.microsoft.com/@$tenantId#/access-packages/$($pkg.id)"

Share the direct URL via an onboarding checklist or identity verification email rather than making the package publicly visible. This prevents casual requests while keeping the request process self-service.
Delegation: Assigning Catalog and Package Roles

The Entitlement Management delegation model has four tiers. An Identity Governance Administrator creates catalogs and can delegate everything below that level to business unit owners without granting them any tenant-wide admin role.

# Assign the Catalog Owner role to a business unit manager
# This grants them full control within the catalog without tenant admin rights
$roleBody = @{
    principalId = '<manager-user-oid>'
    roleDefinitionId = '92e2b946-a4a0-4fb3-a3e7-8ff8e1a10cc3'  # Catalog Owner built-in
    resource = @{
        originId     = '<catalog-id>'
        originSystem = 'AadGroup'
    }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

# Built-in Entitlement Management role IDs:
# Catalog Owner:   92e2b946-a4a0-4fb3-a3e7-8ff8e1a10cc3
# Catalog Reader:  8c8b404d-df32-4bc3-8b4d-cc9c4e98c84c
# Package Manager: 74d97c2f-4a8a-4b34-b1f1-45ca0d86a27a

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/roleAssignments' \
  -Body $roleBody -ContentType 'application/json'

# List all role assignments for a catalog
$assignments = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/roleAssignments?`$filter=resource/originId eq '<catalog-id>'"

$assignments.value | ForEach-Object {
    Write-Host "$($_.principal.displayName): $($_.roleDefinition.displayName)"
}
Create one access package per department with no-expiry policy and manager self-approve for day-1 access. HR triggers request via API or Logic App on hire event.
Create a package with afterDateTime expiration matching the project end date. Set requestorSettings to specific project team members.
Enable autoApplyDecisionsEnabled on quarterly access review with defaultDecision=Deny. Contractors with lapsed projects are removed without manual IT tickets.
Mark Finance Approver and Finance Submitter packages as incompatible. Request blocking is automatic.
Create a hidden package with no approval and direct URL. Share URL only with security team. Audit log captures every use.
Add a connected organization for the partner tenant. Create a policy with scopeType=SpecificConnectedOrganizationUsers and two-stage approval.

# List all active assignments for a specific access package
$assignments = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/assignments?`$filter=accessPackageId eq '<package-id>' and state eq 'Delivered'&`$expand=target,accessPackage"

$assignments.value | Select-Object `
    @{N='User';    E={$_.target.email}},
    @{N='Package'; E={$_.accessPackage.displayName}},
    @{N='Expires'; E={$_.schedule.expiration.endDateTime}},
    state

# Revoke an assignment administratively
$removeReq = @{
    requestType = 'adminRemove'
    accessPackageAssignment = @{ id = '<assignment-id>' }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/assignmentRequests' \
  -Body $removeReq -ContentType 'application/json'

Azure AD Lifecycle Workflows (generally available 2023) can trigger access package assignment requests as part of automated joiner/mover/leaver flows. This removes the self-service step for standard onboarding: the workflow fires on hire date and submits the access package request on behalf of the new user.
Custom Questions on Access Request Forms

Access package policies support custom questions that requestors must answer when submitting a request. Answers are stored with the request and visible to approvers, enabling context-aware approval decisions without requiring a separate ITSM ticket.

Custom question answers are immutable after submission. Approvers see them in the email notification and in the myaccess.microsoft.com approver view. They are also stored in the Entra audit log for compliance evidence.

Access packages do not have explicit versioning — when you add or remove a resource role from a package, the change applies to all new assignments created after that point. Existing active assignments are not retroactively updated. This asymmetry has important operational implications.
Add a resource role to the package
Existing holders do NOT receive the new resource
New requestors receive all current resource roles including the new one
Remove a resource role from the package
Existing holders RETAIN the removed resource until their assignment expires or is revoked
New requestors do not receive the removed resource
Existing assignments are revoked — all resource roles are removed immediately
Existing assignments keep their original end date
New assignments use the updated duration
New requests follow the updated approval flow

If you need to add a new resource to all current holders, you must either use the adminAdd request type for each existing assignment or build an automation that queries all active assignments and updates them via Logic Apps or PowerShell.

# Retroactively grant a new resource to all current holders of a package
# This is necessary because adding a resource to a package does NOT update existing assignments

# 1. Get the new group object ID you added to the package
$newGroupId = '<new-group-oid>'

# 2. Get all active assignments for the package
$assignments = (Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/assignments?`$filter=accessPackageId eq '<pkg-id>' and state eq 'Delivered'&`$expand=target").value

# 3. Add each user directly to the group
foreach ($a in $assignments) {
    $userId = $a.target.objectId
    Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
      -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups/$newGroupId/members/`$ref" \
      -Body (@{ '@odata.id' = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryObjects/$userId" } | ConvertTo-Json) \
      -ContentType 'application/json'
    Write-Host "Added $($a.target.email) to group"
}

Access packages bundle Entra groups, enterprise app roles, and SharePoint site permissions into a single requestable unit — reducing the provisioning workflow for complex resource sets to a single approval.

Multiple policies on one package support different requestor populations (internal vs external, senior vs junior) with different approval requirements and durations, without duplicating resource configuration.
Users can request extensions before expiry; admins can grant extensions directly. Both use the same Graph assignmentRequests API with requestType='userExtend' or 'adminExtend'.
Hidden packages (isHidden=true) remove a package from public catalog browsing; users access them via a direct URL — useful for privileged or sensitive resource bundles.
The four-tier delegation model (Identity Governance Admin → Catalog Owner → Package Manager → Approver) enables business units to self-manage access without tenant admin rights.
All assignment state changes surface to the Entra audit log under the 'Entitlement Management' service, enabling KQL-based compliance queries and SIEM integration.
